text: Duodenal jejunostomy for duodenal obstruction from annular pancreas. ...office with recurrent vomiting and a twenty pound weight loss. He had symptoms of duodenal obstruction and ongoing obstructive symptoms which recurred despite serial dilatation. He remained otherwise constitutionally well and had no major medical comorbidities. Imaging showed the typical appearance of an annular pancreas causing circumferential enveloping of the duodenum. Note the proximal dilated stomach. Annular pancreas is a relatively rare abnormality and a cause of recurrent vomiting in adults. It is an embryological abnormality caused by defective migration of the ventral pancreatic bud which then envelops the second part of the duodenum.

The surgeon places ports, including an 11mm assistant port, for robot docking. A roolim is created in the supracholic compartment, and the mesentery is divided close to the bowel. A linear cutting stapler is used to divide the bowel and confirm the orientation of the root. Scissors with electrocautery create enterotomies on both sides for the jejuno-jejunostomy. A bowel grasper widens the gap, and another firing of the stapler completes the jejuno-jejunostomy. The enterotomy of the common limb is closed with a running stitch followed by a seromuscular stitch.

The surgeon begins dissection in the supracolic compartment, revealing inflammation and adhesions near the gallbladder due to recurrent pancreatitis or duodenal obstruction. The hepatic flexure is widely taken down laparoscopically to facilitate coccurization. Once completed, the duodenum is visible, showing the annular pancreas causing circumferential obstruction. The proximal duodenum appears more dilated and sits without tension as mobilization increases.

It's important to know that annular pancreas does confer a higher than usual risk of pancreatic adenocarcinoma, though this is a relative risk and the absolute risk itself remains small. Historically, annular pancreas was treated with resection, but over time, diversion with the form of a duodenal jejunostomy has thought to be the best option. Gastrojejunostomy does exist as a potential option but is thought to be inferior due to the risk of bioreflux as well as the theoretical risk of a closed-loop syndrome between an intact pylorus and duodenal obstruction from annular pancreas.

The surgeon completes the mobilization of the duodenum, revealing the annular pancreas. The proximal duodenum is positioned without tension and approximated to the previously created Roux limb through a defect in the mesocolon. A two-layer anastomosis is performed using robotic assistance, with a posterior seromuscular row created using 3-0 Vicryl sutures, ensuring an isoperistaltic orientation of the jejunum in the supracolic compartment.

The surgeon uses robotic hock cautery to create enterotomies on both the duodenum and jejunum. The posterior cuff of tissue is stitched first, ensuring the mucosa is grabbed with each bite. Forovacral stitches are used to create a continuous anastomosis, with the anterior wall stitched next, inverting the mucosa using a canal technique.

The surgeon completes the anastomosis using a four ovicral stitch to form another seromuscular layer, analogous to the posterior layer, establishing a two-layer side-to-side functional end-to-end duodenal jejunostomy in the supracolic compartment.
